title: Performance Science2
text:
Deutsch 


  English
Hi! Ich bin Philip - Sportwissenschaftler, Trainer und leidenschaftlicher Sportler. Ich helfe Athlet*innen dabei, ihre Ziele zu erreichen - egal ob im Triathlon, Radsport oder Laufen.

title: Performance Science2
text:
Blog
In meinem Blog teile ich Einblicke in wissenschaftliche Themen, Trainingsratschläge und vieles mehr (auf English).

Zum Blog

title: Performance Science2
text:
Coaching
Egal, welches Ziel du verfolgst - ich erstelle maßgeschneiderte Trainingspläne und biete individuelle Betreuung, damit du dein Ziel erreichst.
Ob erster Triathlon, persönliche Marathon-Bestzeit oder der Ötztaler Radmarathon – ich begleite dich Schritt für Schritt.

Mehr erfahren

title: Performance Science2
text:
Über mich
Leidenschaft trifft auf Expertise. Ich kombiniere praktische Coaching-Erfahrung mit wissenschaftlicher Kompetenz:

Doktorstudium Sportwissenschaft/Leistungsphysiologie
M.Sc. High Performance Sport
C-Trainer Lizenz Triathlon
Langjährige Erfahrung im Hobby-, Nachwuchs- und Profisport

Lese über meine Motivation, meine Philosophie und meine sportliche Leidenschaft.

Lern mich kennen

title: Performance Science2
text:
Deutsch 


  English
Hi! I am Philip - sport scientist, coach and passionate athlete.

title: Performance Science2
text:
Blog
The blog is the heart of my website. Here, I share medium-to-long format insights into scientific topics, training advice and much more.

Go to the blog

title: Performance Science2
text:
Coaching
No matter what goal you’re pursuing - I create tailored training plans and provide individual support to help you achieve it.
Whether it’s your first triathlon, a marathon personal best, or the famous “Ötztaler Radmarathon” - I’ll support you every step of the way.

Learn more

title: Performance Science2
text:
About me
Passion meets expertise. I combine hands-on coaching experience with scientific knowledge:

PhD Student Sport Science/Exercise Physiology
M.Sc. High Performance Sport

Certified C-Level Triathlon Coach

Many years of experience in recreational, junior, and professional sport

Read about my motivation, my philosophy, and my passion for sport.

Get to know me

title: Über mich
text:
Ich bin Philip, Coach, Sportwissenschaftler und jemand, der Sport wirklich lebt. Ob beim Triathlontraining, auf einer Bikepacking-Tour durch die Berge oder beim Wildwasser-Kajakfahren - Bewegung ist nicht nur ein Hobby, sondern ein zentraler Teil meines Lebens.
In meiner Forschung als Sportwissenschaftler beschäfitge ich mich mit Ermüdung und dem Einsatz moderner Wearables im Ausdauertraining, etwa zur Messung der Muskelsauer-stoffsättigung. Diese wissenschaftliche Perspektive kombiniere ich in meiner Arbeit mit langjähriger Praxiserfahrung.
Schon mit 14 Jahren stand ich das erste Mal als Schwimmtrainer am Beckenrand. Seitdem durfte ich Athlet*innen in ganz unterschiedlichen Bereichen begleiten: Vom Kinderschwimmen über Nachwuchs- und Profi-Triathlon bis hin zum Kajak Guide.
Diese Vielfalt prägt meine Arbeit - und genau das gebe ich im Coaching weiter.

title: Über mich
text:
Meine Philosophie
Sport muss vor allem Spaß machen. Ich arbeite gerne sehr ambitioniert und optimiere die letzten Details, um das Beste herauszuholen - aber das ist keine Vorausetzung für meine Athlet*innen. Wenn du Freude an dem hast, was du tust - ganz egal deiner sportlichen Ziele - führt das automatisch zu persönlichem Erfolg.
Deshalb begleite ich Sportler*innen mit ganz unterschiedlichen Zielen. Jede Situation erfordert eine individuelle Herangehensweise – und genau diese Erfahrung bringe ich in mein Coaching ein.

title: Über mich
text:
Wissenschaftliche Arbeit
Als Doktorstudent in der Sportwissenschaft (University of Rome “Foro Italico”) habe ich mich auf Ausdauersport, Leistungsphysiologie- und diagnostik spezialisiert.
Durch mehrere eigene Forschungsprojekte, ein Forschungspraktikum bei Prof. Stephen Seiler - einem der führenden Experten im Ausdauersport - sowie weitere internationale Kooperationen bin ich bestens vernetzt.
Meine Arbeit fokussiert sich darauf, neue Methoden in Diagnostik, Trainingssteuerung und Trainingsmethodik zu entwickeln. Dabei ist mir die Übertragung von wissenschaftlichen Erkenntnissen in die Praxis besonders wichtig.

title: Über mich
text:
Qualifikationen

M.Sc. High-Performance Sport, Universität des Saarlandes
C-Lizenz Triathlon (Leistungssport)
B-Lizenz Fitnesstrainer
DLRG Ausbilder Schwimmen & Rettungsschwimmen

title: Über mich
text: Sportliche Momente

title: Coaching
text:
Leistungen


Trainingsplanung
Individuelle Trainingspläne für dein Ziel - egal ob Laufen, Radfahren oder Triathlon. Ich begleite dich kontinuierlich und passe das Training flexibel an dein Leben und deine Fortschritte an.


Schwimm-Coaching
Techniktraining im Schwimmen - auf Wunsch mit Videoanalyse. Gemeinsam optimieren wir deinen Schwimmstil und machen dich effizienter im Wasser.


Leistungsdiagnostik
Bestimme deine Trainingsbereiche und Leistungsfähigkeit - remote oder vor Ort. Mit dem für dich passenden Test bekommst du präzise Werte, um dein Training optimal zu steuern und Fortschritte zu messen.


Bikefitting
Deine Sitzposition entscheidet über Komfort und Leistung. Wir analysieren und optimieren dein Setup, damit du effizient und beschwerdefrei trainieren kannst.


Experten-Beratung
Ob Training, Schlaf oder Erholung – in einem 30- oder 60-minütigen Gespräch klären wir deine Fragen und finden Lösungen, die dich weiterbringen.

title: Coaching
text:
Pakete und Leistungen


“Essential”
100€ pro Monat
 Monatliche Trainingsplanung
 Monatlicher Video- oder Telefon-Call
 Unbegrenzte Kommunikation über Trainingsplattform
 Trainingsanalyse und Fortschrittskontrolle


“Premium”
200€ pro Monat
 Wöchentliche Trainingsplanung
 Wöchentliche Calls, dynamische Plananpassung
 Unbegrenzte Kommunikation über Trainingsplattform
 Maßgeschneiderte Lösung für Trainingsanalyse und Fortschrittskontrolle


Weitere Leistungen
Preis auf Anfrage
 Leistungsdiagnostik
 Coaching für Profis
 Bikefitting
 Schwimm-Coaching
 Individuelle Beratung

title: Blog
text:
As scientists we are doing our own little research, creating new knowledge and presenting it to our peers. With this blog I am trying to take the next step: Provide research insights and communicate scientific ideas to a broader audience.
Topics will range from research article discussions, opinion pieces and coaching thoughts.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text: Hi, this is Philip on Performance Science2, and this is my first blog post. Have some patience with my language not being as sharp as it could be. I’m expecting (hoping) to get better! In this first blog post I will dive into the idea of scientific training - and why it’s often a misperceived.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
The case

In 2023, when the “Norwegian Hype Train” was moving fast, winning all major races on the way, a young Australian brought the train to an abrupt stop - at least for this moment. Max Neumann won the men’s PTO European Open race and didn’t stop at the finish line. In the post race interview he was shooting against the Norwegians, and attributed his victory to his “no bullshit science” approach (video ref), receiving wide applause. Indeed, the Norwegians are (were) famous for being equipped with dozens of sensor-technology and giving hundreds of drops of blood to measure blood lactate concentration each year. Often this extensive support - only possible with more people involved to analyze the data - is characterized as scientific training.

Maybe the Norwegians never claimed themselves to base their training on science, but the quote from Max Neumann in the post race interview reflects a very common notion: There would be something like scientific training.
Let’s stop here and take a step back. What does science even mean? And does scientific training even exist?

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
What is science?
You may be surprised to read that even scientists don’t agree on a definition of science. Characetistics found in many definitions are:

Science is a system. It is structured in organizations that follow common practices (“rituals”)
Science aims to generate generalizable knowledge (“truths”)
Science tests hypotheses generated from prior knowledge
Science uses specialized methods

There are some parallels with what in sports generally is considered scientific training:

Technology use - While some technology is well-established (e.g heart rate sensors and power meters), novel devices such as core temperature sensors (and pills) or muscle oxygenation monitors are becoming used more frequently. This trend originates in technology transfer and democratization of knowledge.
Personalized insights - technology is used to understand the athlete’s response to training and how they recover in real world conditions.
Precise training control - the insights are then used to execute training sessions and maximize adaptation potential while minimizing recovery needs.

Next to the similarities there are some relevant differences:
Training usually does not aim to generate knowledge applicable beyond a single athlete. When done right, technology can be used to understand how one athlete handles a training session. However, training is performed in varying environments (e.g. hot summer day versus mild spring), complicating generalizability. If we acknowledge that athletes respond differently to training, it becomes clear that it is hard to generate new knowledge.
The final point is about hypothesis testing. Smart coaches and athletes can use technology to test if a new training method resulted in performance gains. There is one problem though: In science, experimental conditions are strictly controlled to test the cause-effect of a single variable. This is unrealistic with real athletes. When preparing for a competition many aspects are changed at once: Nutrition is optimized, different intervals are performed while doing a training block in altitude. Consequently, the athlete will know the combination of all these things leads to a good performance but it is impossible to quantify individual contributions.
As we can see, training can approximate and mimic scientific principles. This assumes that the coach-athlete team really is meaningfully using the data to form hypothesis on training responses and critically evaluates them to create an explicit understanding on the athlete’s training response. From my experience all the tech typically is used to create an implicit understanding of an athlete, their strengths, weaknesses and response characteristics. Yet, it is in the core of science to clearly understand cause-effect relationships. Therefore, I would argue that all the fancy-looking social media content and coaches boasting about being scientific is about one thing: Marketing

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
Evidence-based vs. Evidence-informed training
As mentioned above, science aims to generate general truths. Most research in sport science is conducted to be applicable and guide decision making. The knowledge generated is extensively democratized and leads to drastically improved training programs, better nutrition strategies and understanding of recovery compared to 20 years ago.
When translating scienctific knowledge and applying it in the work with athletes, there is one important aspect to consider: Due to the nature of science it can tell us what will work for most athletes but it doesn’t tell if it the best for one specific athlete. And it does this in a very specific way.
Let’s examplify this: We read a study comparing different
There is a second dimension I didn’t touch on so far. I believe many people claiming to do scientific training are saying their training is evidence-based. But what does that even mean? Read a study comparing different HIT interval protocols and apply the best study? If you are doing this, you’re fallen for a trap: Research use extreme protocols to maximize the primary variance between different protocols. If you base your training on extreme levels, you don’t apply the scientific knowledge, you’re simply replicating what researchers thought would be good to test their hypothesis.
A few years ago X&Y have published a scientific article in journal x (ref), highlighting this misconception. They propose the term evidence-informed as sensible alternative. It puts the athlete, coach or practitioner into the position to synthesize a broad range of scientific literature and then take out the specific aspects they deem relevant for a specific athlete in a specific situation.
I will challenge any coach who claims they provide scientific training or even evidence-based training. Creating an evidence-informed system takes time.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
Motivation

In 2023, when the “Norwegian Hype Train” was moving fast, winning all major triathlon races, a young Australian brought the train to an abrupt halt - at least for a short moment. Max Neumann from Australia won the men’s PTO European Open race and didn’t stop at the finish line. In the post race interview he was shooting against the Norwegians, and attributed his victory to his “no bullshit science” approach (video), receiving wide applause. Indeed, the Norwegian triathletes are famous for training equipped with dozens of sensor-technology and measuring lactate constantly. Often this extensive monitoring program is characterized as scientific training.

Maybe the Norwegians never claimed themselves to do scientific training but the quote from Max Neumann echos a common notion: There is something like scientific training.
Let’s stop here and take a step back. What does science even mean? Does scientific training exist?

Haftung für Inhalte

Die Inhalte unserer Seiten wurden mit größter Sorgfalt erstellt. Für die Richtigkeit, Vollständigkeit und Aktualität der Inhalte können wir jedoch keine Gewähr übernehmen. Nach §§ 8 bis 10 DDG sind wir als Diensteanbieter jedoch nicht verpflichtet, übermittelte oder gespeicherte fremde Informationen zu überwachen oder nach Umständen zu forschen, die auf eine rechtswidrige Tätigkeit hinweisen. Bei Bekanntwerden von entsprechenden Rechtsverletzungen werden wir diese Inhalte umgehend entfernen.

Haftung für Links

Unser Angebot enthält Links zu externen Webseiten Dritter, auf deren Inhalte wir keinen Einfluss haben. Deshalb können wir für diese fremden Inhalte auch keine Gewähr übernehmen. Für die Inhalte der verlinkten Seiten ist stets der jeweilige Anbieter oder Betreiber der Seiten verantwortlich. Die verlinkten Seiten wurden zum Zeitpunkt der Verlinkung auf mögliche Rechtsverstöße überprüft. Rechtswidrige Inhalte waren zum Zeitpunkt der Verlinkung nicht erkennbar. Eine permanente inhaltliche Kontrolle der verlinkten Seiten ist jedoch ohne konkrete Anhaltspunkte einer Rechtsverletzung nicht zumutbar. Bei Bekanntwerden von Rechtsverletzungen werden wir derartige Links umgehend entfernen.

Urheberrecht

Die durch die Seitenbetreiber erstellten Inhalte und Werke auf diesen Seiten unterliegen dem deutschen Urheberrecht. Die Vervielfältigung, Bearbeitung, Verbreitung und jede Art der Verwertung außerhalb der Grenzen des Urheberrechtes bedürfen der schriftlichen Zustimmung des jeweiligen Autors bzw. Erstellers. Downloads und Kopien dieser Seite sind nur für den privaten, nicht kommerziellen Gebrauch gestattet. Soweit die Inhalte auf dieser Seite nicht vom Betreiber erstellt wurden, werden die Urheberrechte Dritter beachtet. Insbesondere werden Inhalte Dritter als solche gekennzeichnet. Sollten Sie trotzdem auf eine Urheberrechtsverletzung aufmerksam werden, bitten wir um einen entsprechenden Hinweis. Bei Bekanntwerden von Rechtsverletzungen werden wir derartige Inhalte umgehend entfernen.

Datenschutz

Die Nutzung unserer Webseite ist in der Regel ohne Angabe personenbezogener Daten möglich. Soweit auf unseren Seiten personenbezogene Daten (beispielsweise Name, Anschrift oder eMail-Adressen) erhoben werden, erfolgt dies, soweit möglich, stets auf freiwilliger Basis. Diese Daten werden ohne Ihre ausdrückliche Zustimmung nicht an Dritte weitergegeben.
Wir weisen darauf hin, dass die Datenübertragung im Internet (z.B. bei der Kommunikation per E-Mail) Sicherheitslücken aufweisen kann. Ein lückenloser Schutz der Daten vor dem Zugriff durch Dritte ist nicht möglich.
Der Nutzung von im Rahmen der Impressumspflicht veröffentlichten Kontaktdaten durch Dritte zur Übersendung von nicht ausdrücklich angeforderter Werbung und Informationsmaterialien wird hiermit ausdrücklich widersprochen. Die Betreiber der Seiten behalten sich ausdrücklich rechtliche Schritte im Falle der unverlangten Zusendung von Werbeinformationen, etwa durch Spam-Mails, vor.

Google Analytics

Diese Website benutzt Google Analytics, einen Webanalysedienst der Google Inc. (‘’Google’‘). Google Analytics verwendet sog.’‘Cookies’’, Textdateien, die auf Ihrem Computer gespeichert werden und die eine Analyse der Benutzung der Website durch Sie ermöglicht. Die durch den Cookie erzeugten Informationen über Ihre Benutzung dieser Website (einschließlich Ihrer IP-Adresse) wird an einen Server von Google in den USA übertragen und dort gespeichert. Google wird diese Informationen benutzen, um Ihre Nutzung der Website auszuwerten, um Reports über die Websiteaktivitäten für die Websitebetreiber zusammenzustellen und um weitere mit der Websitenutzung und der Internetnutzung verbundene Dienstleistungen zu erbringen. Auch wird Google diese Informationen gegebenenfalls an Dritte übertragen, sofern dies gesetzlich vorgeschrieben oder soweit Dritte diese Daten im Auftrag von Google verarbeiten. Google wird in keinem Fall Ihre IP-Adresse mit anderen Daten der Google in Verbindung bringen. Sie können die Installation der Cookies durch eine entsprechende Einstellung Ihrer Browser Software verhindern; wir weisen Sie jedoch darauf hin, dass Sie in diesem Fall gegebenenfalls nicht sämtliche Funktionen dieser Website voll umfänglich nutzen können. Durch die Nutzung dieser Website erklären Sie sich mit der Bearbeitung der über Sie erhobenen Daten durch Google in der zuvor beschriebenen Art und Weise und zu dem zuvor benannten Zweck einverstanden.

title: What is scientific training - and does it exist?
text:
The false claim
So far we discovered that science is defined by specific characteristics that will differ vastly from working with individual athletes. Both worlds exist in paralell and can learn from each other. The scientist looks at what athletes are doing and tries to understand it. The coach takes knowledge and applies it to a training program. They form a strong synergy.
But why do coaches claim to b.
We discovered that there is no scientific training. Still, we can leverage science in the training process. This is typically referred to as evidence-based or evidence-informed training.

title: Evidence based versus evidence informed coaching
text: We discovered that there is no scientific training. Still, we can leverage science in the training process. This is typically referred to as evidence-based or evidence-informed training.

title: Evidence based versus evidence informed coaching
text:

As mentioned above, science aims to generate general truths. Most research in sport science is conducted to be applicable and guide decision making. The knowledge generated is extensively democratized and leads to drastically improved training programs, better nutrition strategies and understanding of recovery compared to 20 years ago.
When translating scienctific knowledge and applying it in the work with athletes, there is one important aspect to consider: Due to the nature of science it can tell us what will work for most athletes but it doesn’t tell if it the best for one specific athlete. And it does this in a very specific way.

There is a second dimension I didn’t touch on so far. I believe many people claiming to do scientific training are saying their training is evidence-based. But what does that even mean? Read a study comparing different HIT interval protocols and apply the best study? If you are doing this, you’re fallen for a trap: Research use extreme protocols to maximize the primary variance between different protocols. If you base your training on extreme levels, you don’t apply the scientific knowledge, you’re simply replicating what researchers thought would be good to test their hypothesis.
A few years ago X&Y have published a scientific article in journal x (ref), highlighting this misconception. They propose the term evidence-informed as sensible alternative. It puts the athlete, coach or practitioner into the position to synthesize a broad range of scientific literature and then take out the specific aspects they deem relevant for a specific athlete in a specific situation.
I will challenge any coach who claims they provide scientific training or even evidence-based training. Creating an evidence-informed system takes time.

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
Introduction
Near-infrared spectroscopy allows to investigate muscle oxygenation non-invasively. The technology has been proposed to complement traditional measures in endurance performance testing by providing insights into peripheral changes. However, there is a lack of longitudinal studies investigating how peripheral muscle oxygen saturation (SmO2) changes with improving fitness and endurance performance. The lack of longitudinal data over long periods such as single or multiple seasons in well-trained athletes is understandable as training interventions are difficult to conduct on sufficiently large samples of competitive athletes. For these instances where data collection is not feasible or even impossible, well-conducted case reports provide unique value to better understand the phenomena of interest (ref).
Here, the SmO2 response of a youth development cyclist during a laboratory based performance test is presented over a period of more than 2 years.

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
Athlete Characteristics
The athlete in this case study is male and was 18 years old at the first test performed in August 2023 and just started training seriously for road cycling in that year. His previous sporting background was in edit before he started cycling at age 16 for fun. Because of his good performances, the athlete quickly became motivated to pursue the sport more seriously.
General athlete charcteristics are presented in Table 1. The athlete performed the same laboratory performance test 4 times over a period of 4 years (for details read “Methods”). Traditional performance measures over the investigation period are summarized in Table 2.



Table 1: General athlete characteristics

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
Methods


Code
# To start the analysis, the data files are loaded.
# load GXT data amd renamae
GXT_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS

GXT_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
GXT_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |> rbind(GXT_Data_clean) -> GXT_Data_clean
}

# Ramp Data preparation
Ramp_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS, then filter with Time >300s

Ramp_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
Ramp_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |>
    group_by(Test) |>
    filter(Time > 300) |>
    mutate(Time = Time -300) |> 
    ungroup() |>
    rbind(Ramp_Data_clean) -> Ramp_Data_clean
}


# load LMT data amd renamae
LMT_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS

LMT_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
LMT_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |> rbind(LMT_Data_clean) -> LMT_Data_clean
}



Procedures
A single visit protocol with 3 separate tests was performed. At arrival, body mass and body fat percentage using the 10-skinfold method ref were assessed and the NIRS probe placed on the vastus lateralis (VL). The athletes own bike was mounted on an indoor bike trainer (Kickr Core, Wahoo, …*) that was regularly calibrated. Then, the exercise test began, which is presented in Figure 1.

Graded Exercise Test
The submaximal graded exercise test (GXT) commenced, starting at 95 W with an increase of 35 W every 5 minutes. Lactate was sampled at the end of each stage. The test was terminated when the expected maximal lactate steady-state (MLSS) was reached, operationalized by a blood lactate concentration [BLa] \(\geq\) 4 mmol/L. The main goal of the GXT was to create the submaximal [BLa] profile and to identify the first lactate threshold (LT1).


Ramp Test
After a rest period of a few minutes the athlete started cycling at 50 W for 5 minutes as recovery. Following one-minute of passive rest, the ramp protocol started at 100 W and increased by 25 W every 30 s. The goal of the test was to establish the peak power output (PPO) and estimate the maximal oxygen uptake (e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\)), hence the steep increase.


Lactate Minimum Test
To obtain a physiology-based estimate of the MLSS (eMLSS), the modified lactate minimum test (mLMT, ref) was performed. Following the ramp test, the athlete was asked to sit passively on a chair for exactly 7 minutes for lactate to evenly distribute across the whole body. Then, the mLMT commenced at 40 W below the expected maximal lactate steady state and increased by 10 W every 90 s. Lactate was sampled at the end of each state. The test was terminated when the athlete reached voluntary exhaustion or when [BLa] rose for two consecutive stages, whichever occurred first.


Code
# to visualize the data from all tests in one figure, combine the data from the 
# GXT, Ramp and LMT from the same Test into one data frame. 
# Adjust the Time variables

end_GXT <- as.numeric(GXT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10" & Power > 10) |> summarize(Time = max(Time)))
end_Ramp <- as.numeric(end_GXT + 300 + Ramp_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10" & Power > 10) |> summarize(Time = max(Time)))

protocol_data <- GXT_Data_clean |>
  filter(Test == "25_10") |>
  bind_rows(
    Ramp_Data_clean |> 
      filter(Test == "25_10") |>
      mutate(Time = Time + end_GXT + 300,
             Power = ifelse(Power > 90, Power, 0)),
    LMT_Data_clean |>
      filter(Test == "25_10") |>
      mutate(Time = Time + end_Ramp + 420,
             Power = ifelse(Power > 200, Power, 0))
    ) |>
  mutate(Time = as.duration(Time))

end_Total <- max(protocol_data$Time)

protocol_data |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, Power)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = Power), fill = clrs[5], alpha = .5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0, end_GXT, end_GXT + 345 ,end_Ramp, end_Ramp+420, end_Total), linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate(geom = "text",
  x = c(0 + 60, end_GXT + 345 + 60, end_Ramp + 420 + 60),
  y = 500,
  label = c("(1) \nGXT","(2) \nRamp","(3) \nLMT"),
  hjust = 0
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,550), breaks = seq(0,500,100)) +
  labs(
    x = "Time [min:sec]",
    y = "Power Output [watt]"
  )





Figure 1: Design of the testing session. After the GXT (1), a ramp test (2) is performed. Following 7 minutes of passive rest, the LMT is started (3)











Measures

Lactate profile and threshold determination
[BLa] was sampled from the earlobe using a portable anaylzer (Lactate plus, …). LT1 was determined using the LTratio method ref. eMLSS is determined as power output at the mathematically derived minimum of the 3rd order polynomial fitted to the eLMT [BLa] data.


e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\)
Because access to a CPET system was not available, expired gases and \(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_2\) could not be directly measured. Therefore, e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\) was calculated from PPO using a linear regression equation. As this value is only an estimate, PPO is reported as well.


Muscle Oxygenation
A wireless and wearable NIRS device (Moxy Monitor, …) was placed on the right VL, approximately halfway between the trochanter major and the lateral patella. The device recorded at a sampling rate of 0.5 Hz with 10-second smoothing. The data was recorded using GoldenCheetah software (ref), which was used to control the indoor trainer in ergometer mode.



Data Processing
The SmO2 data are exported from Golden Cheetah as .csv file for further processing using R software (ref). Raw signals are pre-processed using {mnirs} package (ref). Specifically, a smoothed spline (with spar = 0.05) was applied. No normalization of the data was applied as SmO2 during the first GXT stage was highly similar over time.

GXT SmO2 response
Average SmO2 for each stage was computed as mean. The rate of change in SmO2 (SmO2rate) is determined after excluding the initial 60 s and last 10s of each stage. A first SmO2 breakpoint (BP1) was identified visually as the first sudden change in SmO2 in 1) the raw data, 2) the avergaged SmO2 and 3) in SmO2slope. This was done using a 3-panel plot (Fig X). For raw data, the power was interpolated from the relative completion of the stage.


Ramp SmO2 response
w


LMT SmO2 response
w

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text: Dates of Tests

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
SmO2 response over Time
The full raw data are displayed in Figure 2. Because including all 4 tests make the figures difficult to read, from now on only one test per year will be displayed

Code
raw1 <- GXT_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "SmO~2~ [%]"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = GXT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600)),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "none"
  )

raw2 <- 
Ramp_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time-60, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    color = "Test Date"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = Ramp_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(xmin = min(Time),
                  xmax = max(Time),
                  ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600),
                  group = Test),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "none",
      axis.text.y = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

raw3 <- LMT_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    color = "Test Date"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = LMT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(xmin = min(Time),
                  xmax = max(Time),
                  ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600),
                  group = Test),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,710), breaks = seq(0,720,90)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "inside",
      legend.position.inside = c(.9,.9),
      axis.text.y = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

raw1 
raw2 
raw3

(c) LMT

The second test from November 2023 has been removed in Figure 3 to highlight the changes over a larger time period.

Let’s start with the plots of all timepoints

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
callout: the analytical code is available by clicking on the “code” buttons throughout the case report.
Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
library(lubridate)
library(patchwork)
library(sysfonts)
library(showtext)
library(grid)
library(tidyr)
library(renv)
library(remotes)
#remotes::install_github("jemarnold/mnirs")
library(mnirs)
#renv::init()
#renv::snapshot()

source("../../theme_blog.R")

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
Introduction
Near-infrared spectroscopy allows to investigate muscle oxygenation non-invasively. The technology has been proposed to complement traditional measures in endurance performance testing by providing insights into peripheral changes. However, there is a lack of longitudinal studies investigating how peripheral muscle oxygen saturation (SmO2) changes with improving fitness and endurance performance. The lack of longitudinal data over long periods such as single or multiple seasons in well-trained athletes is understandable as training interventions are difficult to conduct on sufficiently large samples of competitive athletes. For these instances where data collection is not feasible or even impossible, well-conducted case reports provide unique value to better understand the phenomena of interest (ref).
Here, the SmO2 response of a youth development cyclist during a laboratory based performance test is presented over a period of more than 2 years.

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
Athlete Characteristics
The athlete in this case study is male and was 18 years old at the first test performed in August 2023 and just started training seriously for road cycling in that year. His previous sporting background was in edit before he started cycling at age 16 for fun. Because of his good performances, the athlete quickly became motivated to pursue the sport more seriously.
General athlete charcteristics are presented in Table 1. The athlete performed the same laboratory performance test 4 times over a period of 4 years (for details read “Methods”). Traditional performance measures over the investigation period are summarized in Table 2.



Table 1: General athlete characteristics






Value




Sex
Male

178

Measure

November 2025

67.8

71

-
6.9

185

eMLSS [watt]
300

PPO [watt]

e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\) [ml/min/kg]

text:
Methods


Code
# To start the analysis, the data files are loaded.
# load GXT data amd renamae
GXT_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Test_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS

GXT_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
GXT_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |> rbind(GXT_Data_clean) -> GXT_Data_clean
}

# Ramp Data preparation
Ramp_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("Ramp_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS, then filter with Time >300s

Ramp_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
Ramp_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |>
    group_by(Test) |>
    filter(Time > 300) |>
    mutate(Time = Time -300) |> 
    ungroup() |>
    rbind(Ramp_Data_clean) -> Ramp_Data_clean
}


# load LMT data amd renamae
LMT_Data <- rbind(
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2023_08_15.csv"), Test = "23_08"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2023_11_11.csv"), Test = "23_11"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2024_05_01.csv"), Test = "24_05"),
  data.frame(read.csv("LMT_2025_10_25.csv"), Test = "25_10")
              ) |>
  rename(
    Time = secs,
    Cadence = cad,
    HR = hr,
    Power = watts,
    SmO2 = smo2,
    tHb = thb
  ) |>
  select(Time, Cadence, HR,Power, SmO2, tHb, Test)

# apply smoothing with mNIRS

LMT_Data_clean <- data.frame()

for (i in 1:4) {
LMT_Data |>
    filter(Test == unique(Test)[i]) |>
    mnirs::create_mnirs_data(
    nirs_device = "Moxy",
    nirs_channels = "SmO2",
    time_channel = "Time",
    sample_rate = 2
  ) |>
  mnirs::filter_mnirs(
    sample_rate = 1,
    method = "smooth_spline",
    spar = 0.05
  ) |> rbind(LMT_Data_clean) -> LMT_Data_clean
}



Procedures
A single visit protocol with 3 separate tests was performed. At arrival, body mass and body fat percentage using the 10-skinfold method ref were assessed and the NIRS probe placed on the vastus lateralis (VL). The athletes own bike was mounted on an indoor bike trainer (Kickr Core, Wahoo, …*) that was regularly calibrated. Then, the exercise test began, which is presented in Figure 1.

Graded Exercise Test
The submaximal graded exercise test (GXT) commenced, starting at 95 W with an increase of 35 W every 5 minutes. Lactate was sampled at the end of each stage. The test was terminated when the expected maximal lactate steady-state (MLSS) was reached, operationalized by a blood lactate concentration [BLa] \(\geq\) 4 mmol/L. The main goal of the GXT was to create the submaximal [BLa] profile and to identify the first lactate threshold (LT1).


Ramp Test
After a rest period of a few minutes the athlete started cycling at 50 W for 5 minutes as recovery. Following one-minute of passive rest, the ramp protocol started at 100 W and increased by 25 W every 30 s. The goal of the test was to establish the peak power output (PPO) and estimate the maximal oxygen uptake (e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\)), hence the steep increase.


Lactate Minimum Test
To obtain a physiology-based estimate of the MLSS (eMLSS), the modified lactate minimum test (mLMT, ref) was performed. Following the ramp test, the athlete was asked to sit passively on a chair for exactly 7 minutes for lactate to evenly distribute across the whole body. Then, the mLMT commenced at 40 W below the expected maximal lactate steady state and increased by 10 W every 90 s. Lactate was sampled at the end of each state. The test was terminated when the athlete reached voluntary exhaustion or when [BLa] rose for two consecutive stages, whichever occurred first.


Code
# to visualize the data from all tests in one figure, combine the data from the 
# GXT, Ramp and LMT from the same Test into one data frame. 
# Adjust the Time variables

end_GXT <- as.numeric(GXT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10" & Power > 10) |> summarize(Time = max(Time)))
end_Ramp <- as.numeric(end_GXT + 300 + Ramp_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10" & Power > 10) |> summarize(Time = max(Time)))

protocol_data <- GXT_Data_clean |>
  filter(Test == "25_10") |>
  bind_rows(
    Ramp_Data_clean |> 
      filter(Test == "25_10") |>
      mutate(Time = Time + end_GXT + 300,
             Power = ifelse(Power > 90, Power, 0)),
    LMT_Data_clean |>
      filter(Test == "25_10") |>
      mutate(Time = Time + end_Ramp + 420,
             Power = ifelse(Power > 200, Power, 0))
    ) |>
  mutate(Time = as.duration(Time))

end_Total <- max(protocol_data$Time)

protocol_data |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, Power)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = Power), fill = clrs[5], alpha = .5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0, end_GXT, end_GXT + 345 ,end_Ramp, end_Ramp+420, end_Total), linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate(geom = "text",
  x = c(0 + 60, end_GXT + 345 + 60, end_Ramp + 420 + 60),
  y = 500,
  label = c("(1) \nGXT","(2) \nRamp","(3) \nLMT"),
  hjust = 0
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,550), breaks = seq(0,500,100)) +
  labs(
    x = "Time [min:sec]",
    y = "Power Output [watt]"
  )





Figure 1: Design of the testing session. After the GXT (1), a ramp test (2) is performed. Following 7 minutes of passive rest, the LMT is started (3)











Measures

Lactate profile and threshold determination
[BLa] was sampled from the earlobe using a portable anaylzer (Lactate plus, …). LT1 was determined using the LTratio method ref. eMLSS is determined as power output at the mathematically derived minimum of the 3rd order polynomial fitted to the eLMT [BLa] data.


e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\)
Because access to a CPET system was not available, expired gases and \(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_2\) could not be directly measured. Therefore, e\(\dot{\text{V}}\text{O}_{2\text{max}}\) was calculated from PPO using a linear regression equation. As this value is only an estimate, PPO is reported as well.


Muscle Oxygenation
A wireless and wearable NIRS device (Moxy Monitor, …) was placed on the right VL, approximately halfway between the trochanter major and the lateral patella. The device recorded at a sampling rate of 0.5 Hz with 10-second smoothing. The data was recorded using GoldenCheetah software (ref), which was used to control the indoor trainer in ergometer mode.



Data Processing
The SmO2 data are exported from Golden Cheetah as .csv file for further processing using R software (ref). Raw signals are pre-processed using {mnirs} package (ref). Specifically, a smoothed spline (with spar = 0.05) was applied. No normalization of the data was applied as SmO2 during the first GXT stage was highly similar over time.

GXT SmO2 response
Average SmO2 for each stage was computed as mean. The rate of change in SmO2 (SmO2rate) is determined after excluding the initial 60 s and last 10s of each stage. A first SmO2 breakpoint (BP1) was identified visually as the first sudden change in SmO2 in 1) the raw data, 2) the avergaged SmO2 and 3) in SmO2slope. This was done using a 3-panel plot (Fig X). For raw data, the power was interpolated from the relative completion of the stage.


Ramp SmO2 response
w


LMT SmO2 response
w

title: Case Study of changes in SmO2 in a youth development cylcist
text:
SmO2 response over Time
The full raw data are displayed in Figure 2. Because including all 4 tests make the figures difficult to read, from now on only one test per year will be displayed

Code
raw1 <- GXT_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "SmO~2~ [%]"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = GXT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600)),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "none"
  )

raw2 <- 
Ramp_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time-60, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    color = "Test Date"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = Ramp_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(xmin = min(Time),
                  xmax = max(Time),
                  ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600),
                  group = Test),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "none",
      axis.text.y = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

raw3 <- LMT_Data_clean |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    color = "Test Date"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = LMT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(xmin = min(Time),
                  xmax = max(Time),
                  ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/600),
                  group = Test),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = .5) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,710), breaks = seq(0,720,90)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black","grey25","grey50", "grey75"),
                     labels = c("Aug 23", "Nov 23","Mai 24","Oct 25")) +
  theme(
      legend.position = "inside",
      legend.position.inside = c(.9,.9),
      axis.text.y = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  )

raw1 
raw2 
raw3

Submaximal GXT
The second test from November 2023 has been removed in Figure 3 to highlight the changes over a larger time period.


Code
GXT_Data_clean |>
  filter(Test != "23_11") |>
  ggplot(aes(Time, SmO2, color = Test)) +
  labs(
    caption = "SmO~2~ data was smoothed using the {mnirs} package with a smoothed spline (spar = 0.05)",
    x = "Time [seconds]",
    y = "SmO~2~ [%]",
    color = "Test Date"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(data = GXT_Data_clean |> filter(Test == "25_10"),
              aes(xmin = min(Time),
                  xmax = max(Time),
                  ymin = 0,
                  ymax = Power*(100/350),
                  group = Test),
              fill = "grey", 
              color = NA,
              alpha = 0.2, 
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,100), breaks = seq(0,100,20))
